summary: Replaced the hand-written assembly object for MSL mem_funcs with a matching C implementation derived from cam's work, moving MSL/mem_funcs.c from NonMatching to Matching in configure.py and switching obj_files.mk from asm/MSL/mem_funcs.s.o to src/MSL/mem_funcs.c.o. The matched code implements the four low-level copy helpers for aligned, reverse-aligned, unaligned, and reverse-unaligned long copies using MWERKS-only code, pointer auto-increment/decrement idioms, word-copy unrolling, and shift/or reconstruction for unaligned source data.
what_changed: Added a 238-line C implementation in src/MSL/mem_funcs.c for __copy_longs_aligned, __copy_longs_rev_aligned, __copy_longs_unaligned, and __copy_longs_rev_unaligned.
Deleted asm/MSL/mem_funcs.s, which previously contained the assembly implementations for the same four mem_funcs helpers.
Changed RuntimeLib configuration in configure.py so Object(NonMatching, "MSL/mem_funcs.c") became Object(Matching, "MSL/mem_funcs.c").
Changed obj_files.mk so the build uses $(BUILD_DIR)/src/MSL/mem_funcs.c.o instead of $(BUILD_DIR)/asm/MSL/mem_funcs.s.o.

The C implementation is guarded with #if defined(__MWERKS__), uses #pragma ANSI_strict off and #pragma defer_codegen on, and relies on macros that cast the dst/src variables through byte and long pointer views.
smart_moves: Kept the C close to the assembly structure: initial byte alignment fixup, bulk long-copy loops, remainder long-copy loops, and final byte tail copies are all explicit.
Used a deref_auto_inc(p) macro expanding to *++(p) to express PowerPC update-form load/store patterns such as lbzu, lwzu, stbu, and stwu in C-friendly form.
For aligned paths, expressed the 32-byte bulk copy as eight repeated long assignments inside a do/while loop, matching the assembly's unrolled eight-word transfer pattern.
For unaligned paths, reconstructed words with paired shifted long loads using left_shift, right_shift, v1, and v2, mirroring the assembly's slw/srw/or sequence.
Separated forward, reverse, aligned, and unaligned behavior into the same symbol boundaries as the original assembly, allowing the asm file to be removed cleanly once the C object matched.
decomp_lessons: MSL runtime routines may require non-idiomatic C that mirrors CodeWarrior codegen rather than modern valid-looking C style.
Pointer aliases implemented as macros over src and dst can be a practical matching tactic when the original codegen repeatedly reinterprets the same arguments as unsigned char* and unsigned long*.
For low-level memory-copy code, matching often depends on preserving pre-increment/pre-decrement pointer expressions and do/while loops rather than refactoring into clearer index-based loops.
Build metadata must be updated alongside asm-to-C conversions: configure.py matching status and obj_files.mk object paths both need to point at the C object.

Evidence from the PR comment indicates clang/compile_flags checking had trouble with this file: refactoring to more standard C was attempted but did not match, and the compile_flags issue was described as a clang error rather than an ignorable warning.
naming_conventions: Preserved original MSL helper symbol names with double-underscore prefixes: __copy_longs_aligned, __copy_longs_rev_aligned, __copy_longs_unaligned, and __copy_longs_rev_unaligned.

Used compact local names that reflect the assembly/dataflow roles: i for loop counts, v1/v2 for adjacent loaded words, src_offset for byte misalignment, and left_shift/right_shift for unaligned word reconstruction.
Defined short pointer-view macros cps, cpd, lps, and lpd for char-pointer source/destination and long-pointer source/destination.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: #pragma ANSI_strict off was used, indicating the implementation intentionally relies on compiler extensions or non-strict C behavior for matching.
#pragma defer_codegen on was used, a CodeWarrior-specific matching lever present in the new file.
#if defined(__MWERKS__) wraps the actual implementation body, limiting the matching-specific code to the Metrowerks compiler path.
Alignment fixups are computed with expressions equivalent to the assembly clrlwi/neg patterns, e.g. (-(unsigned long) dst) & 3 and ((unsigned long) cpd) & 3.
Forward aligned copying starts byte pointers at src - 1 and dst - 1, then uses pre-increment dereferences to produce update-form instructions.
Reverse copying starts byte pointers at src + n and dst + n, then uses *--pointer forms to match reverse update loads/stores.
Bulk aligned copies use n >> 5 for groups of 32 bytes and (n & 31) >> 2 for remaining words, matching the assembly's 8-word unroll and residual word loop.
Unaligned copies compute src_offset, left_shift = src_offset << 3, and right_shift = 32 - left_shift, then combine adjacent loaded longs with shifts and bitwise OR.
The tail byte copy for unaligned paths adjusts cps by 4 - src_offset or src_offset before copying the final n & 3 bytes, preserving the assembly's source pointer correction behavior.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.summary: Reported that compile_flags.txt issues could not simply be ignored because they produced an actual clang error, not a warning; also did not know how to exclude the file from the check at that moment.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.summary: Tried refactoring the code to be valid C but was not able to keep it matching that way.

follow_up_queries: Find other MSL runtime PRs that use #pragma ANSI_strict off or #pragma defer_codegen on for matching.
Search for deref_auto_inc or similar pointer auto-increment macros in matched runtime code.
Inspect later PRs touching compile_flags.txt or clang checks for src/MSL/mem_funcs.c.
Compare this mem_funcs.c against other CodeWarrior/MSL source reconstructions for canonical naming and pointer idioms.
